content.text: Neural networks do not operate on raw text or categorical labels directly — they require
numeric representations

called embeddings. An embedding maps discrete objects (words, tokens, users, products) into continuous vectors in a high-dimensional space where geometric relationships encode semantic similarity. Every modern LLM and agent pipeline depends on embeddings at multiple stages: token lookup, retrieval, clustering, and similarity scoring.
What it is

An embedding is a learned dense vector that represents an item in a way that preserves useful structure. In language models, each vocabulary token has an embedding vector stored in an embedding matrix. During forward pass, token IDs index into this matrix to produce the input representation fed to transformer layers. Beyond token embeddings, the same idea applies to sentence embeddings, document embeddings, and multimodal embeddings that align text with images or code.
Why it matters

Agents need to compare, retrieve, and route information by meaning — not by exact string match. Embeddings make this possible at scale. RAG systems embed documents and queries into the same space to find relevant context. Router agents embed user intents to select tools. Memory systems embed past interactions for semantic recall. Without understanding embeddings, it is impossible to reason about why similar prompts behave similarly or why retrieval quality depends on the embedding model.
Benefits
Semantic similarity
— cosine distance between vectors approximates meaning overlap
Compact representation
— fixed-size vectors regardless of original text length (for sentence-level embeddings)
Differentiable learning
— embeddings are trainable parameters optimized end-to-end with the model
Transfer across tasks
— pretrained embeddings capture general language structure reusable by agents
Trade-offs
High-dimensional vectors consume memory proportional to vocabulary size times embedding dimension
Static embeddings (Word2Vec-era) cannot represent context-dependent word senses
Embedding quality varies by training data domain — legal embeddings may fail on medical text
Similarity in embedding space is approximate and can miss logical constraints
How it works

Token embeddings start as randomly initialized vectors. During pretraining, the model adjusts these vectors so that tokens appearing in similar contexts end up near each other. The embedding layer is a lookup table: given token ID 42, return row 42 of the matrix.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

vocab_size = 50_000
embed_dim = 768  # typical for base LLMs

embedding_layer = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embed_dim)
token_ids = torch.tensor([[101, 2345, 892, 102]])  # batch of 1 sequence

# Shape: (batch, seq_len, embed_dim)
vectors = embedding_layer(token_ids)
print(vectors.shape)  # torch.Size([1, 4, 768])
For retrieval and agent memory, sentence or document embeddings compress entire texts into one vector using pooling (mean, CLS token, or dedicated encoder):

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
query = "How do I reset my API key?"
docs = [
    "To rotate credentials, open Settings > API Keys.",
    "Our refund policy allows returns within 30 days.",
]

query_vec = model.encode(query, normalize_embeddings=True)
doc_vecs = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True)

# Cosine similarity via dot product on normalized vectors
scores = doc_vecs @ query_vec
best_idx = scores.argmax()
print(f"Best match: {docs[best_idx]}")
When to use it

Use token embeddings whenever interacting with an LLM — they are internal to every model call. Use dedicated embedding models when building RAG indexes, semantic search, clustering user intents, deduplicating agent memory, or ranking tool descriptions by relevance to a query.
How to implement
For agent RAG pipelines, choose an embedding model matched to your domain and latency budget. Index documents in batches, store vectors in a vector database, and embed queries at retrieval time.

from openai import OpenAI

client = OpenAI()
text = "Agent policy: always confirm destructive actions."

response = client.embeddings.create(
    model="text-embedding-3-small",
    input=text,
)
vector = response.data[0].embedding
print(len(vector), vector[:3])  # 1536 dims for this model
Examples

Two sentences with different words but similar meaning — "How do I cancel my subscription?" and "I want to stop paying monthly" — produce embedding vectors with high cosine similarity. Conversely, sentences sharing words but different intent — "Apple released a new phone" versus "Apple pie recipe" — should diverge in a well-trained embedding space.
Real-world example

A customer-support agent indexes 120,000 help articles using text-embedding-3-small (1536 dimensions). Each article chunk is 512 tokens. At query time, the agent embeds the user message, retrieves the top-5 chunks by cosine similarity, and injects them into the prompt. Switching from a general embedding model to one fine-tuned on support tickets improved retrieval precision from 71% to 84% without changing the LLM.
Common mistakes
Using the LLM's internal token embeddings for retrieval instead of a dedicated embedding model
Ignoring normalization when computing cosine similarity
Assuming high similarity implies factual equivalence — embeddings capture semantic overlap, not truth
Mixing embedding models between indexing and query phases
Best practices
Normalize embedding vectors before dot-product similarity search
Use the same model and preprocessing for index and query
Evaluate retrieval with domain-specific labeled query-document pairs
Monitor embedding drift when re-indexing after model upgrades
Comparison with alternatives

One-hot encoding represents each token as a sparse vector with a single 1 — no similarity between different tokens. Embeddings are dense and learned. TF-IDF captures term frequency but not deep semantics. Contextual embeddings from transformer hidden states (or dedicated encoders) outperform static Word2Vec-style vectors on most agent retrieval tasks.
Mental model

Think of embeddings as coordinates on a semantic map. Items with nearby coordinates are related in meaning. The model learns the map layout during training; agents navigate it to find, compare, and cluster information.
Key takeaways
Embeddings convert discrete tokens and texts into numeric vectors that encode meaning
LLMs use token embeddings internally; agents use sentence/document embeddings for retrieval and routing
Cosine similarity on normalized vectors is the standard comparison metric
Embedding model choice directly affects RAG and semantic search quality
